name: Blacksmith
summary: A metalworker who forges and repairs iron and steel tools, hardware, and structural pieces using heat and hammer work.
duties: Heat metal in a forge, shape it on an anvil, weld and join parts, repair agricultural and household metal goods, and sometimes shoe horses in smaller communities.
skills: Metalworking, heat control, tool maintenance, design of functional objects, physical stamina.
tools: Forge, anvil, hammer, tongs, bellows; modern shops add welders and grinders.
regions: Worldwide historically; still present in rural and heritage contexts.

events.label: Industrial competition
events.description: Mass-produced factory metal goods reduce demand for village smiths in many regions.

sources.title: Historical craft surveys
sources.note: Origin estimate aggregated from archaeological metalwork traditions.

name: Cooper
summary: A craft worker who built and repaired wooden barrels, casks, and tubs bound with hoops for storage and transport.
duties: Shape staves, assemble vessels, fit hoops, seal containers for liquids and dry goods, and maintain reusable shipping packaging.
skills: Wood selection, joinery, measurement, knowledge of liquids and swelling wood.
tools: Drawknife, adze, hoop driver, windlass, cooper's axe.
regions: Europe, Americas, and port cities worldwide through the 19th century.

events.label: Decline of wooden shipping
events.description: Steel drums, cardboard, and palletized logistics largely replace cooperage in industry.

name: Scribe
summary: A literate copyist and record-keeper who produced manuscripts and official documents before widespread printing and literacy.
duties: Copy texts, maintain ledgers, draft contracts, assist temples and courts with written records.
skills: Handwriting, grammar, archival organization, sometimes accounting or legal formulae.
tools: Reed or quill pens, ink, parchment or papyrus, rulers, wax tablets.
regions: Ancient Egypt, Mesopotamia, medieval Europe, Islamic scholarly centers, and East Asia.

events.label: Printing press spreads
events.description: Movable type accelerates document production; scribal guilds shrink in Europe.

name: Typist
summary: An office worker who produced typed correspondence and forms on typewriters, central to 20th-century business administration.
duties: Type letters, reports, and forms; manage carbon copies; sometimes take shorthand dictation.
skills: Touch typing, formatting, proofreading, office workflow.
tools: Typewriter, carbon paper, correction fluid, later word processors.

events.label: Word processing
events.description: Digital text editing reduces dedicated typing pools in many offices.

name: Data Entry Clerk
summary: A worker who inputs structured information into computer systems, successor to much typist and clerical data work.
duties: Enter records into databases, validate fields, scan documents, support billing and logistics forms.
skills: Keyboarding, attention to detail, basic software literacy, data quality checks.
tools: PC, scanners, ERP and CRM forms, spreadsheets.
educationPath: High school plus on-the-job training; some roles prefer associate degrees.
modernSectors: Healthcare admin, logistics, finance back-office, BPO.

name: Telegraph Operator
summary: A specialist who sent and received Morse code messages over telegraph wires, enabling rapid long-distance communication in the 19th and early 20th centuries.
duties: Transmit messages, route traffic through relay stations, maintain logs, troubleshoot line issues with field crews.
skills: Morse proficiency, listening comprehension, accuracy under time pressure.
tools: Telegraph key, sounder, wire lines, relay equipment.
regions: North America, Europe, colonies, and rail networks worldwide.

events.label: Telephone and radio rise
events.description: Voice and wireless systems compete with telegraph traffic.

name: Plague Doctor
summary: A physician contracted during plague outbreaks in early modern Europe, often associated with beaked masks and municipal pest control.
duties: Treat bubonic plague patients, record deaths, perform autopsies in some cities, advise quarantines.
skills: Herbal medicine of the period, public health reporting, negotiation with city officials.
tools: Beaked mask, waxed robe, cane, herbal pouches.
regions: Italian city-states and broader Europe, especially 17th century.

name: Software Developer
summary: A professional who designs, writes, tests, and maintains computer programs and systems that run on digital hardware.
duties: Implement features, review code, debug production issues, collaborate on architecture, document APIs and behavior.
skills: Programming, algorithms, version control, testing, system design, communication.
tools: IDEs, Git, cloud platforms, databases, CI pipelines.
regions: Global; concentrated in tech hubs and remote-first companies.
educationPath: Computer science degrees, bootcamps, and self-directed learning are all common paths.
modernSectors: SaaS, finance, healthcare IT, gaming, government, embedded systems.

events.label: Web era
events.description: Internet applications explode demand for application developers.

events.label: Mobile platforms
events.description: Smartphones create new client and backend specializations.

sources.title: Computing history timelines
sources.note: Origin year marks early professional programming roles.

name: Computer Programmer
summary: A specialist who writes instructions for computers, closely related to the software developer role with overlapping duties in many organizations.
duties: Code business logic, maintain legacy systems, optimize performance, integrate libraries.
skills: Languages, debugging, data structures, domain modeling.
originLabel: World War II era computing projects
educationPath: STEM degrees and vocational training programs.

name: Clerk
summary: A worker who keeps records, handles correspondence, and supports administrative workflows in organizations.
duties: File documents, process forms, assist customers or officials, maintain schedules and inventories.
skills: Literacy, numeracy, organization, customer service, software use in modern roles.
tools: Ledgers historically; today PCs, spreadsheets, and ERP modules.
regions: Worldwide across public and private institutions.

name: Brewer
summary: A maker of beer through mashing, fermentation, and finishing, from household ale wives to industrial brewery operators.
duties: Select grains and hops, manage fermentation, ensure sanitation, blend batches to style.
skills: Microbiology basics, recipe design, quality control, equipment operation.
tools: Mash tuns, fermenters, kettles, hydrometers, modern PLC-controlled plants.
regions: Global; ancient Mesopotamia to contemporary craft and industrial breweries.
